

| Gemini Enterprise Demo  ([Jamie de Guerre](mailto:jamiedeguerre@google.com)) In this demo I’ll first walk you through the Vertex AI Agent Platform and some of the governance features for IT administrators.  Then, I’ll show some of the new features coming to the Gemini Enterprise app at NEXT.  And finally, I’ll show an end-to-end customer scenario for a bank that is automating loan request processing.  Lets start with the Agent Platform. In this admin UI I have full access to all the tools I need to build, scale, govern, and optimize agents across my company.  In Deployments I see all my running agents, and can drill into analytics, sessions, or traces.  From the Registry I can manage which agents are running, what identity each runs under, and who can access the agent. And, from the MCP Server Registry I can do the same for all of the tools my agents are using. We are adding a third registry for skills early this summer.  I can also govern policies for my agents, including my gateway policies. This is crucial as with thousands of agents running across my organization, with access to all of our enterprise data, and tools to take actions across our systems, I need to ensure that all communication is effectively secured and there is no risk of data loss on egress.  1:30 2:00  | ![][image1] |
| :---- | :---- |
|  Next, lets look at the Gemini Enterprise app built on top of this platform.  The app leverages all the governance capabilities of the underlying platform, giving IT teams full control, but providing employees with a simple interface that hides all the complexity.  Across all our customers, increasingly we see that customers want to run long running agents that don’t just make a single LLM call with a custom prompt and some additional context. Instead, these agents are given a sandbox environment in which they can write code to create their own tools, compile and run that code, evaluate their own results and iterate until the agent is confident it is achieving the outcome with high quality.  As customers switch to these long-running agents the UI paradigm shifts from synchronous chat to employees managing a team of agents and only interacting with those agents when they have updates or need input.  For this we are introducing **Inbox**. Where employees can manage all the updates from their team of agents, either through Gemini Enterprise directly or by configuring their notifications to be sent to the mobile app, gChat, Slack, or email.  We are also adding:  An **Agents Marketplace**, pre-built **Agent Templates**, **Skills** and **Collaborative Projects**. So those are some of the new features coming to the Gemini Enterprise app.  Next, lets look at the end-to-end customer scenario in the current released version of the app.  1:40 \- 3:10  3:30  | ![][image2] |
|  For this scenario, we will look at Meridian National Bank and their agentic approach to automating loan processing.  Mortgage underwriting is a data-intensive process requiring the synthesis of PII, credit telemetry, and risk-weighting based on loan-to-value ratios. Banking is highly regulated and compliance requirements make this very challenging to do with AI. We cannot rely on a purely probabilistic approach; we need a hybrid architecture that offers both agentic reasoning and deterministic policies to enforce rigid regulatory guardrails. This is precisely what the new **Gemini Agent Designer** provides.  0:45 \- 3:55 4:10 | ![][image3] |
|  Let's start by asking Gemini Enterprise to review pending loan applications. **Prompt:**   **Give the latest 10 unprocessed mortgage loan applications**  Gemini Enterprise is able to automatically detect that I have an agent available uniquely capable of answering this question and so it proactively offers to orchestrate calls to this agent. \[Confirm\]  I can now see all the applications that are dynamically pulled from the inbox. 0:30 \- 4:30  | Feature showcase: Agent Orchestration  ![][image4]  |
| Let’s call our powerful agent and let’s ask it to process the L\_0001 application. **Prompt:**  **Process application L\_0001**  0:15   | ![][image5] (Don’t wait for it to load, continue, you come back to this screen after the output is generated) |
|  \[Switch to ‘Agent Engine’ \- [link](https://console.cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/agents/agent-engines/locations/us-central1/agent-engines/5524026132539637760/playground?project=demo4events10) \- show playground\]  Now, because the Gemini Enterprise app is built on top of the Agent Platform, as an IT administrator I can drill into what’s happening on the backend by going to the admin console. Here I can see the active session, and the sub-agent steps that the agent is calling to process this loan.  I could also drill into traces, evals, and more.  0:30 \- 5:15  | ![][image6] Feature showcased: Real-time observability  |
| \[Returning to Gemini Enterprise, next tab showing completed request\] \[Click Agents on the left tab and click the ‘Loan Execution Agent’ in My Agents\]  Now, in addition to leveraging the agentic reasoning of this full code agent, my loan team would also like to add oversight and guardrails through deterministic policies. And they can do that from our no-code agent designer.  Employees can create new agents just by asking in natural language. And our plan is to make these agents be long running agents that leverage a sandbox, write their own tools, etc.  Employees can also use a flow-based editor to explicitly specify deterministic logic. So in the case of loan processing, that might look something like this. Here we have an workflow that runs on a regular schedule, leverages the full-code agent for agentic processing of the new loan requests, and then takes one of four different actions based on if the loan was approved, rejected, or needs human review.  As an employee I can test this out right here.  0:45 \- 6:00  |  Feature Showcase: Workflow Agent with Orchestration  ![][image7] |
| As an employee I can test this out right here.  \[Click Preview \> Start manually\] This flow pulls applications from the inbox and automatically calls the Loan Supervisor agent we just ran. Depending on the agent’s answer, either an auto email is sent or human input is requested.  \[Switch to next tab with already run\] In this case, you can see it was flagged for human review. The loan request has a risk score of 6, and the agent has flagged concerns with an employment gap and a large unexplained bank deposit.  \[ REJECT\] In this case I’ll reject this request, which causes a ticket to be created in ServiceNow and an email to be sent to the loan officer.  0:45 \- 6:45   | ![][image8]  ![][image9]  |
| \[Switch to next tab \- email\] Here’s the email, and if I click the link you can see the new ServiceNow ticket that was created.  0:30 \- 7:15  |  ![][image10]  ![][image11] |
|  OK \- so that is a good example of how our customers are currently combining full code long running agents with deterministic logic. Next, lets go back to Gemini Enterprise and look at how we can report on the results of loan processing for the past week.  \[Click on the conversation you last created\] Prompt:  **Summarize the loan applications processed in the last batch**  \[Switch to next tab\] \[Click HTML report\]  0:45 \- 8:00   |   ![][image12]  ![][image13] |
| Now let's create a deck for the above information.  \[Select Canvas\]   Prompt: **Generate a slide deck with this report**  \[Switch to next tab \- finished presentation\]   0:30 \- 8:30 |   ![][image14] |
| And from here, we can open this presentation in Workspace.    |  |
